questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\), what is the product \(\mathbf{AB}\)?
questions.hint: Multiply row of A by column of B: \((1)(3) + (2)(4)\).
questions.explanation: \(\mathbf{AB} = (1)(3) + (2)(4) = 3 + 8 = 11\). Option D incorrectly presents it as a \(1\times1\) matrix instead of a scalar, but mathematically \(11\) is correct as a real number.

source_trace.content_anchor: product of row and column matrix

explanations_tiered.definition: Matrix multiplication of a \(1 \times n\) row matrix by an \(n \times 1\) column matrix yields a scalar (a \(1 \times 1\) matrix).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The product is computed by multiplying corresponding entries and summing them: \(\sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{1k} b_{k1}\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply the first number of A by the first number of B, then the second by the second, and add them up.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For \(\mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{1\times n}\) and \(\mathbf{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times 1}\), the product is the dot product \(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}\), a scalar in \(\mathbb{R}\).
reasoning_steps: Check compatibility: columns of A (2) = rows of B (2).
Sum the products.
misconceptions: Some incorrectly multiply entry-wise without summing, giving \(\begin{pmatrix}3 & 8\end{pmatrix}\).

glossary_entry.definition: The product of a row and column matrix of compatible size is the sum of the products of corresponding entries.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An operation producing a scalar when a row matrix multiplies a column matrix of equal length.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that the result is a scalar, not a matrix with brackets necessarily.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{B} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\), compute \(\mathbf{AB}\).
questions.hint: Row 1 of A times each column of B gives row 1 of AB.
questions.explanation: \(AB = \begin{pmatrix}1\cdot0+2\cdot2 & 1\cdot1+2\cdot3 \\ 3\cdot0+4\cdot2 & 3\cdot1+4\cdot3\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}4 & 7 \\ 8 & 15\end{pmatrix}\). Option B miscalculates the top-left entry as \(1\cdot0+2\cdot1=2\).

explanations_tiered.definition: Matrix multiplication: \((AB)_{ij}\) is the dot product of row \(i\) of \(A\) and column \(j\) of \(B\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For \(2\times2\) matrices, multiply rows of first by columns of second and sum the products.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Take each row of A, slide it across each column of B, multiply matching numbers and add.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The entry \(c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{2} a_{ik} b_{kj}\) gives the linear combination of columns of \(B\) weighted by row \(i\) of \(A\).
reasoning_steps: Multiply row 1 of A by column 1 of B: \(1\cdot0+2\cdot2=4\).
Multiply row 1 of A by column 2 of B: \(1\cdot1+2\cdot3=7\).
Multiply row 2 of A by column 1 of B: \(3\cdot0+4\cdot2=8\).
Multiply row 2 of A by column 2 of B: \(3\cdot1+4\cdot3=15\).

glossary_entry.definition: The \(ij\)-th entry is the dot product of row \(i\) of the first matrix and column \(j\) of the second.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The product of two matrices computed as dot products of rows and columns.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show arrow diagrams mapping rows to columns to illustrate the process.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: What is the identity matrix of order 2, denoted \(I_2\)?
questions.hint: It has 1s on the main diagonal and 0s elsewhere.
questions.explanation: The identity matrix \(I_2 = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) satisfies \(AI_2 = A = I_2A\) for any \(2\times2\) matrix \(A\). Option C is the zero matrix, which does not preserve multiplication.

explanations_tiered.definition: The identity matrix \(I_n\) is a square matrix with 1s on the main diagonal and 0s elsewhere.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The identity matrix acts as the multiplicative identity: any matrix multiplied by \(I\) remains unchanged.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The identity matrix is like the number 1 for matrix multiplication; it has 1s down the diagonal and 0s elsewhere.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(I_2 = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) satisfies \(AI_2 = A\) and \(I_2A = A\) for any \(2\times2\) matrix \(A\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The identity matrix \(I_n\) is the unique matrix such that \(AI_n = I_nA = A\) for all \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\), representing the identity linear transformation.
reasoning_steps: Recall that the identity matrix has 1s on the diagonal.
All off-diagonal entries are 0.
Match to the given options.
misconceptions: Some confuse identity matrix with zero matrix or with the matrix of all ones.

glossary_entry.definition: A square matrix with 1s on the main diagonal and 0s elsewhere.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Square matrix with 1s on diagonal, 0s elsewhere.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that \(I\) is the multiplicative identity for matrices.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Identity matrix has 1s on main diagonal, 0s elsewhere.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.2.6 Transpose of a Matrix and Its Properties
questions.question: If \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{pmatrix}\), what is the transpose \(\mathbf{A}^t\)?
questions.explanation: The transpose converts rows to columns: row1 \((1,2,3)\) becomes column1, row2 \((4,5,6)\) becomes column2. Option C incorrectly rearranges without respecting original row order.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.2.6 Transpose of a Matrix and Its Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: The transpose of an \(m \times n\) matrix \(A\) is the \(n \times m\) matrix \(A^t\) with \((A^t)_{ij} = a_{ji}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Transpose flips rows and columns: the first row becomes the first column, the second row becomes the second column, etc.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Flip the matrix over its diagonal: rows become columns.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Transposition is an involution: \((A^t)^t = A\), and it reverses multiplication order: \((AB)^t = B^t A^t\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write first row of A as first column of \(A^t\): \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}\).
Step 2: Write second row of A as second column of \(A^t\): \(\begin{pmatrix}4\\5\\6\end{pmatrix}\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the number of rows (2) and columns (3) of A.
\(A^t\) will have 3 rows and 2 columns.
Place each row of A as a column of \(A^t\).
misconceptions: Some rotate the matrix instead of reflecting it, producing \(\begin{pmatrix}3&6\\2&5\\1&4\end{pmatrix}\).

glossary_entry.definition: The matrix obtained by interchanging rows and columns of the original matrix.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the transpose of a \(2\times3\) matrix?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A \(3\times2\) matrix where rows become columns.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Matrix formed by exchanging rows and columns.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a concrete example to show the flipping process.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Transpose converts \(2\times3\) to \(3\times2\) with rows as columns.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.2.6 Transpose of a Matrix and Its Properties
questions.question: For matrices \(\mathbf{A}\) and \(\mathbf{B}\) where \(\mathbf{AB}\) is defined, which property relates the transpose of the product to the product of transposes?
questions.hint: The transpose reverses the order of multiplication.
questions.explanation: The correct property is \((AB)^t = B^t A^t\). Option A incorrectly preserves order; multiplication of matrices is not commutative, so order reversal is necessary.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.2.6 Transpose of a Matrix and Its Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: Transpose of a product: \((AB)^t = B^t A^t\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The transpose of a product reverses the order of multiplication, analogous to \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}\) for inverses.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you transpose a product, you flip the order and transpose each matrix.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \((AB)^t = B^t A^t\): the transpose of the product equals the product of the transposes in reverse order.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This property holds because \((AB)^t_{ij} = (AB)_{ji} = \sum_k a_{jk} b_{ki} = \sum_k (B^t)_{ik} (A^t)_{kj} = (B^t A^t)_{ij}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the transpose definition: rows become columns.
Consider the \((i,j)\) entry of \((AB)^t\).
It equals the \((j,i)\) entry of \(AB\), which is row \(j\) of \(A\) times column \(i\) of \(B\).
This is the same as row \(i\) of \(B^t\) times column \(j\) of \(A^t\), giving \((B^t A^t)_{ij}\).
misconceptions: Many assume \((AB)^t = A^t B^t\) by analogy with numbers, forgetting that matrix multiplication order matters.

glossary_entry.definition: The transpose of a matrix product equals the product of the transposes in reverse order.

flashcard.front: What is \((AB)^t\) in terms of \(A^t\) and \(B^t\)?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Property reversing multiplication order under transposition.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}\) to reinforce the pattern.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \((AB)^t = B^t A^t\), reversing the multiplication order.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A square matrix \(\mathbf{A}\) is symmetric if:
questions.hint: Symmetric means equal to its transpose.
questions.explanation: A symmetric matrix equals its own transpose: \(A^t = A\). Option B defines a skew-symmetric matrix, where \(A^t = -A\).

explanations_tiered.definition: A symmetric matrix satisfies \(A^t = A\), meaning \(a_{ij} = a_{ji}\) for all \(i, j\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Symmetric matrices are symmetric about the main diagonal; the entry in row \(i\), column \(j\) equals the entry in row \(j\), column \(i\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: A symmetric matrix looks the same when flipped over its diagonal.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Symmetric: \(A^t = A\), so \(a_{ij} = a_{ji}\) for all \(i, j\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Symmetric matrices represent self-adjoint linear operators with respect to the standard dot product, having real eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors.
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of transpose.
If \(A^t = A\), the matrix is unchanged after flipping rows and columns.
This is the definition of symmetry.
misconceptions: Some confuse symmetric with diagonal; symmetric matrices can have nonzero off-diagonals as long as \(a_{ij}=a_{ji}\).

glossary_entry.definition: A square matrix equal to its own transpose.

flashcard.front: What condition defines a symmetric matrix?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How is a symmetric matrix defined?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Matrix equal to its transpose; symmetric about diagonal.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show examples where \(a_{12}=a_{21}\) to illustrate symmetry.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. A symmetric matrix satisfies \(A^t = A\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following matrices is skew-symmetric?
questions.hint: For skew-symmetric, \(A^t = -A\) and diagonal entries are 0.
questions.explanation: Skew-symmetric requires \(A^t = -A\). For option A, \(A^t = \begin{pmatrix}0&-2\\2&0\end{pmatrix} = -A\). Option B is symmetric, not skew-symmetric.

explanations_tiered.definition: A skew-symmetric matrix satisfies \(A^t = -A\), implying \(a_{ij} = -a_{ji}\) and \(a_{ii}=0\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In a skew-symmetric matrix, the transpose equals the negative of the matrix, forcing zeros on the diagonal and opposite signs off the diagonal.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Skew-symmetric matrices have zeros on the diagonal, and the top-right and bottom-left entries are opposites.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Skew-symmetric matrices represent alternating bilinear forms and have purely imaginary eigenvalues (or zero).
worked_solution: Step 3: Verify diagonal entries are zero (necessary condition).
Step 4: Option A satisfies both conditions.
reasoning_steps: Skew-symmetric requires \(a_{11}=a_{22}=0\). Eliminate B, C, D.
misconceptions: Some think any matrix with zeros on the diagonal is skew-symmetric, but off-diagonals must be opposites.

glossary_entry.definition: A square matrix satisfying \(A^t = -A\), with zeros on the diagonal and \(a_{ij}=-a_{ji}\).

flashcard.front: What property defines a skew-symmetric matrix?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that diagonal entries must be zero for skew-symmetric matrices.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Given \(\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\), a student claims \(-\mathbf{A} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}\). Is this correct? If not, why?

options.B: No, the bottom-left entry should be -1
options.C: No, the top-right entry should be 1
options.D: No, the bottom-right entry should be 0

questions.hint: Multiplying by -1 changes the sign of every entry.
questions.explanation: \(-\mathbf{A}\) means multiply every entry by \(-1\): \(2\to-2, 1\to-1, -1\to1, 0\to0\). The student's answer matches this exactly, so it is correct.

explanations_tiered.definition: The additive inverse \(-\mathbf{A}\) is obtained by multiplying every entry of \(\mathbf{A}\) by \(-1\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(-\mathbf{A}\) changes the sign of each entry in the matrix.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Flip the sign of every number in the matrix: positive becomes negative, negative becomes positive.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scalar multiplication by \(-1\) is the additive inverse operation in the vector space \(\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that \(-\mathbf{A} = (-1)\mathbf{A}\).
Multiply each entry by -1.
Compare result with student's answer.
misconceptions: Some think only the first row or column changes sign.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Matrix obtained by multiplying all entries by -1.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this as a quick check for understanding sign changes.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(-A\) multiplies every entry by -1, giving \(\begin{pmatrix}-2&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: 3.2.6 Transpose of a Matrix and Its Properties
questions.question: If \(\mathbf{A}\) is a symmetric matrix, what can be concluded about \(\mathbf{A}^t\)?
questions.hint: Symmetric means equal to its own transpose.
questions.explanation: By definition, a symmetric matrix satisfies \(A^t = A\). Option B describes skew-symmetric matrices, not symmetric ones.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.2.6 Transpose of a Matrix and Its Properties

explanations_tiered.definition: A symmetric matrix equals its transpose: \(A^t = A\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a symmetric matrix, transposition does not change the matrix at all.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a matrix is symmetric, flipping it over the diagonal leaves it exactly the same.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Symmetric matrices are invariant under the transpose involution: \(A^t = A\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of a symmetric matrix.
It directly states \(A^t = A\).

glossary_entry.definition: Matrix equal to its own transpose.

flashcard.front: If A is symmetric, what is A^t?

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. By definition, symmetric means \(A^t = A\).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: For which condition is the product \(\mathbf{AB}\) defined?

options.A: Number of rows of A equals number of rows of B
options.B: Number of columns of A equals number of rows of B
options.C: Number of columns of A equals number of columns of B
options.D: Number of rows of A equals number of columns of B

questions.explanation: If \(A\) is \(m \times p\) and \(B\) is \(p \times n\), then \(AB\) is defined. The number of columns of \(A\) must equal the number of rows of \(B\). Option D reverses this requirement.

explanations_tiered.definition: The product \(AB\) is defined if and only if the number of columns of \(A\) equals the number of rows of \(B\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Compatibility for multiplication requires the inner dimensions to match: \((m \times p) \cdot (p \times n) = (m \times n)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The number of columns in the first matrix must match the number of rows in the second.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: If \(A\) is \(m \times p\) and \(B\) is \(p \times n\), then \(AB\) is defined (size \(m \times n\)).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Matrix multiplication represents composition of linear transformations \(T_B: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^p\) and \(T_A: \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}^m\), requiring the codomain of \(T_B\) to match the domain of \(T_A\).
reasoning_steps: Check the dimensions of A (rows × columns).
Check the dimensions of B (rows × columns).
The product is defined if columns(A) = rows(B).
misconceptions: Many think rows must match rows, or columns must match columns.

glossary_entry.definition: Condition that the number of columns of the first matrix equals the number of rows of the second for multiplication.

flashcard.front: When is the product AB defined?
flashcard.back: When columns of A = rows of B.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What condition must hold for AB to be defined?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Columns of A must equal rows of B.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Condition for matrix multiplication: columns of first = rows of second.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the m×p · p×n = m×n visual to reinforce.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(AB\) is defined when columns of A equal rows of B.
